title: Willow trug basket
subtitle: A flat-bottomed oval garden carrier in brown willow
excerpt: A classic English trug basket in brown willow with a flat oval base, gently bowing sides, and a central handle bow. Used for carrying cut flowers, herbs, and vegetables from the garden. An intermediate willow project that builds border-finishing and handle-fitting skills.
sourceNotes: Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the English trug form and its garden uses. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for willow-work construction principles. Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on Sussex-pattern trugs and garden baskets.

glossaryTerms.definition: Willow basketry weave in which a single rod is woven over and under alternate stakes.
A tight basketry weave that strengthens a transition point such as the base-to-side change or the rim.
For basketry: freshly soaked or freshly cut willow with enough moisture to be pliable without snapping.
A pointed steel rod with a wooden handle, for opening a path through the weave to insert a new stake or to pack down a row.

content.text: The English trug is a long oval garden basket with a high central handle bow and gently sloping sides. Brown willow (willow dried in its bark) gives the right combination of strength and flexibility for the ribs and weaving rods. A finished trug is roughly 45 cm long, 25 cm wide, and 20 cm to the top of the handle.

Willow must be soaked before working. Dried rods that have been in storage need one week of soaking for each year of storage, roughly. Freshly cut willow from the current season needs only twelve to twenty-four hours. Soak the rods submerged in the trough; rods that float out need a weight to keep them under.
Staking the base

The trug base is an oval stake-and-strand construction. Lay seven base stakes across (the long axis) and five slath stakes in the opposite direction. Pair-weave with two thin rods around the slath for three rows to lock the base open. Then rand with single rods, packing each row firmly down with the
bodkin
, until the base is the right size.
Add a row of
waling
at the outer edge of the base to hold the stakes together. The waling row is the boundary between base and side.
Staking up the sides

Cut the base stakes down to 5 cm stubs and insert new side stakes alongside each base stub. Bend each side stake upright at 90 degrees to the base using a swift, clean bend: wet the stake at the base point with a damp cloth, then bend with both thumbs at the bend point while supporting the rod.
Tie the tops of the stakes together temporarily to hold them vertical while you begin the side weaving.
Weaving the sides
Use
randing

with single rods: over one stake, under one, around the basket. The trug sides are not vertical; they flare slightly outward. Guide the flare by angling the stakes outward at 10 degrees from vertical as you establish the first three rows.
Continue randing to the required height (15 cm from base to rim). Pack each row down firmly with the rapping iron. Consistency of packing density determines whether the sides stay even.
The border

Work a three-rod border to close the top. Take each stake, bend it down behind the next stake to the right, then weave in a three-step sequence before trimming the end. The three-rod border leaves a clean, tight finish that is strong enough to carry weight.
The handle bow

Select a thick rod (8 to 10 mm diameter) for the bow. Soak it for an extra twelve hours to make it pliable. Insert one end down through the weave at the centre of one end of the basket, then arch the rod over and insert the other end into the corresponding position at the opposite end. The bow should arch 20 cm above the rim.

Wrap two or three thin rods around the bow from one end to the other in a tight spiral wrap, pushing each wrap down closely against the previous one. Tuck the end under the wrap at the final insertion point.
Finishing

Trim all stake ends and weaving ends flush with secateurs. Let the basket dry in a well-ventilated space for two weeks before heavy use. A new trug will shrink slightly as it dries; this tightens the weave rather than loosening it.
Variations
Smaller herb trug: 30 cm long. Reduce the number of base stakes by two.
Side handles instead of a bow: fit two side loops rather than a central bow for a flat-carried version.

rows.symptom: Base not oval, it has become round
rows.cause: Base stakes not held at the correct spacing during staking
rows.fix: Check the stake spacing after the first three weaving rows and correct before continuing. Use a card template cut to the target oval dimensions.

rows.symptom: Sides flare too much or too little
rows.cause: Stake angle not set consistently during the first rows
rows.fix: At the third row of side weaving, press each stake to the correct angle with your palm and hold it while weaving that row. The weave locks the angle in place.
